journal-meta.journal-id: Int J Cardiol Cardiovasc Risk Prev
Int J Cardiol Cardiovasc Risk Prev

journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Cardiology. Cardiovascular Risk and Prevention

title-group.article-title: Effects of PCSK9 inhibitors on vascular function, lipid profile, and cardiovascular outcomes in patients with peripheral artery disease: A systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Internal Medicine, Al-Kindy College of Medicine, University of Baghdad, Baghdad, Iraq
bDepartment of Internal Medicine, College of Medicine, University of Kufa, Najaf, Iraq
cDepartment of Internal Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, Tanta University, Tanta, Egypt
dDepartment of Internal Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, Menoufia University, Menoufia, Egypt
eDepartment of Internal Medicine, College of Medicine, University of Baghdad, Baghdad, Iraq
fDepartment of Internal Medicine, University of Khartoum Faculty of Medicine, Khartoum, Sudan
gDepartment of Cardiology, West Virginia University, Camden Clark Medical Center, Parkersburg, WV, USA

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author. The Dome, 11111 Alkasr Road, Department of Internal Medicine, University of Khartoum Faculty of Medicine, Khartoum, Sudan. mohammedbadi020@gmail.com

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Peripheral artery disease (PAD) reflects systemic atherosclerosis driven by dyslipidemia, particularly elevated LDL-C. Despite first-line statin therapy, many patients fail to reach lipid targets or are intolerant, necessitating alternatives. We conducted a meta-analysis to assess proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9) inhibitors in PAD.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A systematic search in PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, Web of Science, and Scopus was done up to August 2025. Studies evaluated effects on surrogate vascular markers [ankle-brachial index (ABI), flow-mediated dilation (FMD), carotid intima-media thickness (IMT), lipid profile] and clinical outcomes [major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE), revascularization, amputation, myocardial infarction (MI), and mortality].

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Six studies, assessing 6059 patients were included. PCSK9 inhibitors significantly reduced carotid IMT, LDL-C, total cholesterol, and triglycerides compared with placebo. Based on the available studies, no significant effects were observed on ABI, FMD, or HDL. Clinically, PCSK9 inhibitors lowered the risk of MACE (RR = 0.76, 95% CI [0.60-0.97]), major amputation (RR = 0.38, 95% CI [0.15-0.95]), and MI (RR = 0.63, 95% CI [0.5, 0.79]). Revascularization rates and all-cause mortality were not significant.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: PCSK9 inhibitors may provide lipid-lowering and vascular benefits in patients with PAD, reducing cardiovascular and limb events. While their impact on hemodynamic parameters and mortality remains uncertain, these findings support PCSK9 inhibitors may be effective adjunctive therapy in high-risk PAD populations. These findings support PCSK9 inhibitors may be an effective adjunctive therapy for atherosclerotic risk reduction in high-risk PAD populations. Prospective trials dedicated to limb-specific outcomes are now warranted.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Peripheral arterial disease (PAD) is a common manifestation of systemic atherosclerosis, characterized by narrowing of arteries outside the coronary arteries and the aorta. This arterial narrowing reduces blood flow to peripheral tissues and organs, predisposing them to ischemic complications [1]. PAD represents a substantial global health burden. Although the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) 2021 estimates that over 113 million individuals worldwide are affected by PAD [2], a recent study projected a 220% increase in this prevalence by 2050 [3]. This alarming trend underscores the urgent need for effective therapeutic strategies that reduce mortality and morbidity and improve patients’ quality of life. Despite medical and surgical advances, the complications of PAD remain insufficiently controlled, leaving patients exposed to severe outcomes or, at best, persistent reduction in quality of life [4].

Dyslipidemia, especially LDL-C elevation, is a major modifiable risk factor for peripheral artery disease (PAD), primarily due to its role in promoting atherosclerosis [5,6]. Large epidemiological studies have consistently shown a strong association between dyslipidemia and PAD, with patients exhibiting a higher prevalence and severity of lower extremity arterial obstruction [7,8]. Moreover, lipid-lowering therapies not only reduce cardiovascular morbidity and mortality in patients with PAD but also improve walking performance and limb outcomes, highlighting the central role of lipid management in both prevention and treatment [[9], [10], [11]].

Statins remain the cornerstone of lipid-lowering therapy and have consistently demonstrated a reduction in cardiovascular risk [12]. Nevertheless, many patients with PAD fail to achieve LDL-C targets or experience statin intolerance [[13], [14], [15]], underscoring the need for adjunctive lipid-lowering therapy.

Proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9) inhibitors are a relatively new class of agents that markedly reduce LDL-C levels by enhancing the recycling of LDL receptors in the liver [16]. Beyond their established cardiovascular benefits, there is increasing evidence that PCSK9 inhibition may play a role in improving outcomes in patients with PAD [17,18]. Understanding their potential impact on disease progression, limb outcomes, and cardiovascular risk is crucial for optimizing the management of this high-risk population. This systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to evaluate the impact of PCSK9 inhibitors on patients with PAD, focusing on both surrogate markers of atherosclerotic burden and clinically relevant outcomes. By synthesizing the available evidence, this study sought to clarify whether PCSK9 inhibition translates into measurable improvements in lipid parameters and inflammatory or vascular biomarkers and ultimately reduces adverse cardiovascular and limb events in the high-risk population.

sec.title: Materials and methods
sec.p: This study was conducted according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines [19]. The review was prospectively registered with PROSPERO (registration number: CRD420251135565).

sec.title: Search strategy and information sources
sec.p: A comprehensive literature search was conducted across PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane Library, Web of Science and Scopus from their inception until August 2025. The search strategy incorporated both exploded subject headings and Medical Subject Headings (MeSH), using Boolean operators (AND, OR) to combine key terms such as "PCSK9 inhibitor,” "peripheral artery disease,” and “ankle-brachial index.” This structured approach ensured the inclusion of all relevant studies within the specified time frame. Research strategy provided in Table S1.

sec.title: Study selection and eligibility criteria
sec.p: This review included randomized controlled trials (RCTs), non-randomized trials, and prospective and retrospective cohort studies that evaluate the effects of PCSK9 inhibitors in patients with peripheral artery disease (PAD) on surrogate vascular markers (ABI, FMD, carotid IMT, TCPO2, and lipid profile) and clinical outcomes (Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events (MACE), revascularization, major amputation, myocardial infarction, and death). Eligible studies involved adults (≥18 years) with diagnosed PAD (based on ABI <0.9, imaging, or clinical diagnosis) and were in English. Studies without a defined PAD subgroup or patients with acute limb ischemia without chronic PAD were excluded. Two independent reviewers screened the titles and abstracts of all retrieved articles, with discrepancies resolved through full-text review and discussion with a senior reviewer.

sec.title: Data extraction and management
sec.p: Data extraction was independently performed by two investigators using Microsoft Excel Sheets. The extracted data included study characteristics (study design, country, sample size, intervention, comparator, and follow-up duration), patient demographics (age, sex, past medical history, surgical history, smoking status), and clinical outcomes (Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI), Flow-Mediated Dilation (FMD), Carotid Intima-Media Thickness (IMT), lipid profile (LDL-C, HDL-C, TG, TC), and complications). The extracted data were cross-verified by a senior reviewer for accuracy.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The methodological quality of the included studies was evaluated using established tools appropriate for the study design. For randomized controlled trials (RCTs), the revised Cochrane risk-of-bias tool (RoB 2) [20] was applied to assess potential bias across domains such as randomization, deviations from intended interventions, missing outcome data, measurement of outcomes, and selective reporting. For observational cohort studies, quality was appraised using the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale (NOS) [21], which evaluates the selection of participants, comparability of study groups, and adequacy of outcome assessment. Discrepancies between reviewers were resolved through discussion, and consensus was reached for all assessments.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Given the expected clinical and methodological diversity across studies, including variations in PAD severity, baseline lipid levels, follow-up duration, and outcome assessment methods, a random-effects model using the DerSimonian-Laird estimator was selected for all analyses to provide conservative, generalizable estimates. This approach accounts for both within-study and between-study variance, unlike a fixed-effects model, which assumes a single true effect size across studies.

Data synthesis was performed using Review Manager (RevMan version 5.4.1) [22]. Comparisons between the treatment and control groups were performed using a random-effects model to account for potential heterogeneity across studies. Data were extracted as means (SD) or events and totals. For studies that did not report SDs of change from baseline, we imputed SDs from the reported confidence intervals of the mean differences, assuming equal variances across arms, according to the Cochrane Handbook guidance [23]. When necessary, medians were converted to means (SD) using Hozo et al.'s equation (2005) [24]. Mean differences (MD) and risk ratios (RR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) were calculated. Heterogeneity was assessed using the I2 statistic and Cochran's Q-test. If I2 was <50% and p > 0.1, heterogeneity was considered low. Sensitivity analysis using the leave-one-out method was applied to explore sources of significant heterogeneity (I2 > 50% and p < 0.1). This analysis was only performed when three or more studies contributed to an outcome, as the removal of a single study from a pool of two would preclude a pooled re-estimation. Publication bias was not formally assessed due to the limited number of included studies (n = 6), as such assessments are unreliable with fewer than 10 studies.

sec.title: Search results and study selection
sec.p:
The initial database search identified a total of 786 records (PubMed: 135, Embase: 308, Scopus: 180, Web of Science: 77, Cochrane: 86). After removing 610 duplicates, 176 records remained for screening. Of these, 160 records were excluded after title and abstract review. A total of 16 full-text articles were assessed for eligibility. After detailed evaluation, 10 studies were excluded for the following reasons: abstract or conference proceeding without available full text (n = 2), studies without a defined PAD subgroup or involving acute limb ischemia without underlying chronic PAD (n = 7), and non-English publication (n = 1) (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
PRISMA flowchart.Fig. 1

sec.title: Characteristics of included studies
sec.p:
A total of six studies, enrolling 6059 patients, were included in this meta-analysis. Five of these were randomized controlled trials (RCTs) conducted in the United States [18,[25], [26], [27], [28]], while one was a cohort study conducted in Japan [29]. Four studies evaluated Evolocumab [18,[27], [28], [29]], and two investigated Alirocumab [25,26] as PCSK9 inhibitors in patients with peripheral artery disease(Table 1). provides a summary of the included studies, and (Table 2) outlines their baseline characteristics. The mean age of participants ranged from 63 to 74 years, with the majority being male. Cardiovascular risk factors and comorbidities were highly prevalent, including hypertension (81.3%–100%), diabetes mellitus (44%–100%), and coronary artery disease (19%–92.9%). Most patients were receiving statin therapy prior to enrollment, and a considerable proportion were on high-intensity regimens. Baseline LDL cholesterol levels were elevated across all studies, ranging from 82.7 to 109 mg/dL.Table 1
Summary of included studies.Table 1
Study IDDesignRegionFollow-up durationInterventionControlNumber of patients in intervention groupNo. of patients in. controlTotalPatients inclusion criteriaPrimary outcomesResults
Clavijoa, 2023RCT, Double-blindUSA6 monthsEvolocumab 420 mg SC q4wplacebo353570Patients aged 40–85 years with a diagnosis of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and PAD (Rutherford class I-VI), confirmed by an ankle-brachial index (ABI) ≤0.9 at rest or ≤0.8 after exercise, angiography, duplex ultrasound, or a history of lower extremity surgical or endovascular revascularization. Patients had to be at least one month from their most recent intervention, stable on maximal tolerated lipid-lowering therapy for at least four weeks, and have a fasting LDL cholesterol ≥55 mg/dL or non-HDL cholesterol ≥80 mg/dL.MWT at 6 monthsAfter six months, Evolocumab significantly improved MWT, FMD, Carotid IMT, and plasma MRP-14 levels. In contrast, changes in pain-free walking time, ABI, TBI, transcutaneous oxygen pressure, oxLDL, and sCD36 were not statistically significant.
Sato, 2020PO, Single centerJapan18 ± 11 monthsEvolocumab, 140 mg SC q2w or 420 mg, SC q4wplacebo141630Patients with CLTI were eligible if they had either ischemic foot rest pain with ABI <0.4, ankle pressure <50 mmHg, toe pressure <30 mmHg, or SPP <30 mmHg, or if they presented with a foot ulcer or gangrene accompanied by significant PAD. Those considered for evolocumab had to be on high-intensity statin therapy, have at least one major cardiovascular risk factor (such as CAD, stroke, CKD, or diabetes), and an LDL-C level ≥70 mg/dL12-month freedom from major amputationAfter 12 months, evolocumab significantly improved amputation-free survival, wound-free limb salvage, and lipid parameters (LDL-C, non-HDL-C, MDA-LDL). Conversely, changes in freedom from major amputation, overall survival, and ABI were not statistically significant.
Dhillon, 2025RCT, Double-blindUSA6 monthsEvolocumab, 420 mg SC q4wplacebo131427Patients were aged >40 to <85 years and had a diagnosis of CLTI, Rutherford Class IV–VI. CLTI was confirmed by a toe pressure ≤30 mmHg in non-diabetic patients or ≤40 mmHg in diabetic patients, or by angiography, duplex ultrasound, or a history of lower extremity surgical or endovascular revascularization for CLTI. All participants were required to be on a maximally tolerated statin dose for at least one month prior to enrollment.LDL-C reduction at 3 and 6 months, with platelet activation/reactivity and antithrombotic effectsAfter six months, evolocumab significantly reduced LDL-C, total cholesterol, carotid IMT, and improved FMD. However, changes in ABI, TBI, TcPO2, foot perfusion mapping, wound healing, and most biomarkers (MRP-14, oxLDL, sCD36, VEGF) were not statistically significant.
Bonaca, 2018sub-analysis to FOURIER TrialNorth America, Europe, South America, Asia, Australia, South Africa, and the Middle East2.5 yearsEvolocumab,140 mg SC q2w or 420 mg SC q4wplacebo13,78413,78027,564Patients with clinically evident atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, with PAD, were eligible if they had intermittent claudication with an ankle-brachial index (ABI) < 0.85, a history of peripheral artery revascularization, or an amputation due to atherosclerotic disease. All participants were required to be on statin therapy.MACEs (CV death, MI, stroke, hospitalization for unstable angina, or coronary revascularization)After 2.5 years, evolocumab significantly reduced CV death, MI, stroke, unstable angina, or coronary revascularization. Moreover, changes in CV death, all-cause mortality, major amputation, and urgent revascularization were not statistically significant
Rrapo-Kaso, 2023RCT, Double-blindUSA12 monthsAlirocumab 150 mg SC q2wplacebo181735Patients age 35-85 years old with a clinical diagnosis of peripheral artery disease (PAD). ankle–brachial index (ABI) between 0.4 and 0.9 and a low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) level >70 mg/dL. Participants had to be on maximally tolerated statin therapy for at least 6 months or be statin intolerant.mean change in SFA plaque volume over 1 yearAfter one year, alirocumab significantly reduced LDL cholesterol compared with placebo. However, changes in SFA plaque volume, calf muscle perfusion 6-min walk distance, were not statistically significant
Jukema, 2019RCT, Double-blindUSA3 yearsAlirocumab 75 mg SC q2w or 150 mg SC q2wplacebo373386759Patients were eligible if they were ≥40 years old and had been hospitalized with an acute coronary syndrome (myocardial infarction or unstable angina) within the previous 1 to 12 months. They were required to have elevated atherogenic lipoproteins despite intensive statin therapy, defined as LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL, non–HDL-C ≥100 mg/dL, or apolipoprotein B ≥ 80 mg/dL. All patients were treated with high-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg daily) or their maximum tolerated dose.MACEs (coronary heart disease death, nonfatal myocardial infarction, fatal or nonfatal ischemic stroke, or unstable angina requiring hospitalization).After 3 years, alirocumab significantly reduced the MACE) in the overall population. For all-cause mortality, alirocumab significantly reduced deaths in the overall population

LDL cholesterol98.2 ± 33.199.7 ± 3989.2 ± 19.582.7 ± 37.5--81.7 ± 55.9495.3 ± 36.06--108 ± 32106 ± 41
Total cholesterol173.6 ± 38.0175.8 ± 54.3157.1 ± 28.4152.4 ± 52.2--152.1 ± 66.6168.9 ± 38.82--177 ± 38181 ± 47
HDL cholesterol44.3 ± 13.644.4 ± 12.245.0 ± 9.743.6 ± 12.2--45.5 ± 12.3840.9 ± 11.09----
Triglycerides162.6 ± 86.2158.1 ± 113.8105.7 ± 54.5104.8 ± 58.8--125.2 ± 60.99164.6 ± 93.40--131 ± 57149 ± 85

The included studies exhibited notable clinical heterogeneity. The spectrum of PAD severity ranged from stable claudication (e.g., Bonaca 2018, Rrapo-Kaso 2023) to chronic limb-threatening ischemia (e.g., Dhillon 2025, Sato 2021). The intervention varied between evolocumab (4 studies) and alirocumab (2 studies), with different dosing regimens (140 mg every 2 weeks or 420 mg every 4 weeks). Follow-up duration also varied substantially, from 6 months to 3 years. Methodological heterogeneity was present in study design (5 RCTs, 1 cohort study) and in the assessment techniques for surrogate markers like flow-mediated dilation.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The risk of bias for the included randomized controlled trials was assessed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool Table S2. Three studies (Clavijo 2023, Rrapo-Kas 2023 and Dhillon 2025) were judged to have “some concerns” primarily due to issues with the randomization process or reporting bias. In contrast, Bonaca 2018 and Jukema 2019 demonstrated low risk of bias across all domains. The single included cohort study (Sato 2021) was evaluated using the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale (NOS) Table S3. It scored 8 out of 9 stars, reflecting high methodological quality. The study was strong in terms of selection (representativeness of exposed cohort, selection of non-exposed cohort, and ascertainment of exposure), comparability, and outcome assessment (adequate follow-up duration and completeness of follow-up).

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p:
The pooled effect size showed no significant difference between PCSK9 Inhibitors and Placebo group in terms of change of ankle-brachial index from baseline to the treatment endpoint [6 months] (MD = −0.06, 95% CI [−0.17, 0.06], p = 0.34) (Fig. 2A). Pooled studies were homogenous (P = 0.88; I2 = 0%).2.
Flow-mediated Dilation (FMD)Fig. 2
Forest plot of the pooled Mean Difference MD (A. Ankle-brachial Index (ABI), B. Flow-mediated Dilation (FMD)%, C. Carotid Intima-media Thickness (IMT) (mm)).Fig. 2

The pooled effect estimate showed no significant difference between PCSK9 Inhibitors and Placebo group regarding change of flow-mediated dilation from baseline to the treatment endpoint [6 months] [MD = 1.29, 95% CI [−0.08, 2.66], p = 0.06] (Fig. 2B). Pooled studies were heterogeneous (P < 0.1); I2 = 98%) however, heterogeneity couldn't be resolved due to the limited number of the included studies.3.
Carotid Intima-media Thickness (IMT)

The pooled effect size showed a significant difference between PCSK9 Inhibitors and Placebo group in terms of change of carotid intima-media thickness (IMT) from baseline to the treatment endpoint [6 months] [MD = −0.07, 95% CI [−0.14, −0.00], P = 0.04] (Fig. 2C).). Pooled studies were heterogeneous (P < 0.1); I2 = 98%) however, heterogeneity couldn't be resolved due to the limited number of the included studies.

sec.title: LDL
sec.p:
The pooled effect size of selected studies showed that PCSK9 Inhibitors significantly lower serum LDL compared to Placebo [MD = −47.08, 95% CI [−65.93, −28.23], P < 0.05] (Fig. 3A). Pooled studies were heterogeneous (P < 0.1; I2 = 79%). Heterogeneity was best resolved by omitting Clavijo et al., 2023 [5] (P = 0.65; I2 = 0%) without significant change in the pooled analysis [MD = −37.65, 95% CI [−50.37, −24.93], P < 0.05] (Table 3). The exclusion of a study with some concerns in randomization and reporting bias strengthened the robustness of the LDL-lowering effect.Fig. 3
Forest plot of the pooled Mean Difference MD of Lipid Profile (A. LDL, B. HDL, C. Total Cholesterol, D. Triglycerides).Fig. 3Table 3
Sensitivity analysis results using the leave-one-out method.Table 3
Study excludedMD [95% CI]Total participants (PCSK9 Inhibitor/Placebo)HeterogeneityTest for overall effect
LDH

Triglycerides
Total−16.36 [-59.29, 26.57](63/66)df = 2 (P = 0.04); I2 = 69%P = 0.46
Clavijo et al., 20234.93 [-51.89, 61.76](31/31)df = 1 (P = 0.26); I2 = 20%P = 0.86
Rrapo-Kaso et al., 2023−33.87 [-46.36, −21.38](45/49)df = 1 (P = 0.69); I2 = 0%P < 0.00001

∗Sensitivity analysis was performed only for outcomes with significant heterogeneity (I2 > 50%, p < 0.1) and at least three contributing studies. For outcomes with only two studies (e.g., FMD, carotid IMT, HDL, MACE), sensitivity analysis was not feasible.

sec.title: HDL
sec.p: The pooled effect size of selected studies showed no significant difference between PCSK9 Inhibitors and Placebo group regarding change in serum HDL [MD = 2.63, 95% CI [−6.52, 11.78], P = 0.57] (Fig. 3B). Pooled studies were heterogeneous (P < 0.1); I2 = 74%). However, heterogeneity couldn't be resolved due to the limited number of the included studies.

sec.title: Total cholesterol
sec.p: The pooled effect size of selected studies showed that PCSK9 Inhibitors significantly lower total serum cholesterol level compared to Placebo [MD = −56.84, 95% CI [−85.65, −28.04], P < 0.05] (Fig. 3C). Pooled studies were heterogeneous (P < 0.1); I2 = 81%). Heterogeneity was best resolved by omitting Clavijo et al., 2023 [5] (P = 0.49; I2 = 0%) without significant change in the pooled analysis [MD = −40.30, 95% CI [−60.36, −20.24], P < 0.05] (Table 3).

sec.title: Triglycerides
sec.p: The initial pooled effect size of selected studies showed no significant difference between PCSK9 Inhibitors and Placebo group in terms of change in serum triglycerides level [MD = −16.36, 95% CI [−59.29, 26.57], P = 0.46] (Fig. 3D). Pooled studies were heterogeneous (P < 0.1); I2 = 69%) f. Heterogeneity was best resolved by omitting Rrapo-Kaso et al., 2023 [4], (P = 0.69; I2 = 0%) changing the pooled analysis to demonstrate a significant decrease in serum triglycerides in PCSK9 Inhibitors group compared to Placebo [MD = −33.87, 95% CI [−46.36, −21.38], P < 0.00001]. (Table 3).

sec.title: Major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE)
sec.p:
The pooled effect size of selected studies showed that PCSK9 Inhibitors significantly lower major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) serum cholesterol level compared to Placebo [RR = 0.76, 95% CI [0.60, 0.97], P = 0.02] (Fig. 4. A). However, pooled studies showed moderate heterogeneity (P = 0.1); I2 = 62%). However, heterogeneity couldn't be resolved due to the limited number of the included studies.Fig. 4
Forest plot of the pooled Risk Ratio (RR) of Adverse Events (A. Major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE), B. Revascularization, C. Major Amputation, D. Myocardial Infarction (MI), E. All-cause Mortality).Fig. 4

sec.title: Revascularization
sec.p: The pooled effect size of selected studies showed that there is no significant difference between PCSK9 Inhibitors and Placebo in terms of undergoing revascularization procedures [RR = 1.11, 95% CI [0.97, 1.28, P = 0.13 (Fig. 4. B). Pooled studies were homogenous (P = 0.5; I2 = 0%).

sec.title: Major amputation
sec.p: The pooled effect size of selected studies showed that PCSK9 Inhibitors significantly lower major amputations compared to Placebo [RR = 0.38, 95% CI [0.15, 0.95], P = 0.04] (Fig. 4. C). Pooled studies were homogenous (P = 0.77; I2 = 0%). However, this finding is based on a limited number of events and includes studies, suggesting cautious interpretation.

sec.title: Myocardial infarction (MI)
sec.p: The pooled effect size of selected studies showed that PCSK9 Inhibitors significantly reduce myocardial infarction (MI) risk compared to Placebo [RR = 0.38, 95% CI [0.15, 0.95], P = 0.04] (Fig. 4. D). Pooled studies were homogenous (P = 0.33; I2 = 0%). Similar to other clinical outcomes, this result should be interpreted in light of the limited event data and the inclusion of studies with some risk-of-bias concerns.

sec.title: All-cause mortality
sec.p: The pooled effect size of selected studies showed there is no significant difference between PCSK9 Inhibitors and Placebo in terms of reducing all-cause mortality [RR = 0.83 [0.68, 1.03], P = 0.09] (Fig. 4. E). Pooled studies were homogenous (P = 0.37; I2 = 4%).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis aims to evaluate the potential benefits and limitations of PCSK9 inhibitors on vascular function, lipid Profile, and cardiovascular outcomes in patients with peripheral artery disease (PAD). Our analysis included six controlled trials with a total 6059 patients with a follow-up duration of at least 6 months.

The synthesis of data delineates a significant reduction in carotid intima-media thickness (IMT), serum LDL, total cholesterol and triglycerides concomitant with improved clinical outcomes; including a marked reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE), major amputation and myocardial infarction (MI) in PCSK9 inhibitors group compared to placebo group. It is important to contextualize that the current evidence primarily stems from prespecified analyses of PAD subgroups within large cardiovascular outcome trials. This synthesis therefore provides strong support for the use of PCSK9 inhibitors to reduce atherosclerotic risk in patients with PAD who reflect the high-risk, comorbid profiles of these trials. Notably, these benefits were observed in the absence of any statistically significant difference in ankle-brachial index (ABI) or flow-mediated dilation (FMD) between groups. It is important to note that these particular analyses included a limited number of studies. Therefore, while this dissociation may suggest a therapeutic mechanism centered on systemic atherosclerotic plaque stabilization rather than rapid hemodynamic alteration, this interpretation requires confirmation in larger, dedicated studies. Ankle-brachial index (ABI) provides a functional assessment of blood pressure reduction across arterial stenoses in the lower limbs [30]. The absence of significant change following PCSK9 inhibitor therapy suggests that the profound lipid-lowering achieved does not acutely reverse fixed, fibrocalcific obstructions characteristic of advanced PAD. This finding is attributed to the pathology of atherosclerosis, in which mature plaques exhibit extensive fibrosis and calcification rendering them resistant to rapid regression [31]. This pathophysiological understanding is supported by imaging studies, such as the ASTEROID trial, which showed that even high-intensity statin therapy over 24 months induced only modest regression in the percent atheroma volume measured by intravascular ultrasound [32]. Thus, clinical implication of PCSK9 inhibitors in PAD likely resides not in ameliorating established hemodynamic compromise but in halting the progression of atherosclerosis and stabilizing vulnerable plaques [33].

PCSK9 inhibitors showed no statistical significance regarding improvement in flow-mediated dilation (FMD) compared to placebo. These findings suggest that short-term PCSK9 inhibitions may not yield consistent measurable changes in endothelial function in PAD patients. However, another study by Schremmer et al. (2023) reported that long term therapy with PCSK9 inhibitors significantly improved endothelial function, arterial stiffness and microvascular function, suggesting benefits that extend beyond lipid lowering [34]. The considerable heterogeneity (I2 = 98%) in our analysis likely reflects methodological variations in FMD assessment across studies, a known challenge in this field [35].

In contrast, the significant reduction in carotid intima-media thickness (IMT) provides compelling evidence of a direct anti-atherosclerotic effect. Carotid IMT is a validated surrogate marker of subclinical disease burden, and its progression is a robust predictor of future cardiovascular events [36]. The observed regression aligns with the fundamental principle established in statin trials: the magnitude of LDL reduction is linearly correlated with the slowing of atherosclerotic progression. This structural benefit is likely mediated through multiple pathways beyond lipid lowering, including attenuation of vascular inflammation and promotion of a more stable plaque phenotype, thereby reducing the risk of rupture and thrombosis [37].

The substantial statistically significant reduction in LDL constitutes the principal pharmacodynamic action of PCSK9 inhibitors and serves as the foundation for their therapeutic benefit, this result aligns with the findings of large cardiovascular trials, SPIRE trial [38], which reported mean LDL reductions exceeding 50%. The consistency of this effect across diverse patient populations, including this PAD cohort, reinforces the role of PCSK9 inhibitors as a cornerstone therapy for achieving stringent lipid targets as advocated by contemporary international guidelines [39].

While the benefits of intensive LDL-C lowering for atherosclerotic risk reduction are well-established, it is important to integrate broader lipid-cardiovascular dynamics into the clinical interpretation. Recent evidence has highlighted potential paradoxes, such as the relationship between lipid levels and atrial fibrillation (AF). Specifically, a meta-analysis of nearly 4 million patients found that higher total cholesterol levels were inversely associated with AF risk, and notably, increased LDL-C levels were associated with a 7% reduction in the development of AF (HR = 0.93; p = 0.04) (Yao et al., 2023). Although these findings are characterized by high heterogeneity, addressing such paradoxes strengthens the contextual understanding of aggressive lipid modulation in patients with complex cardiovascular phenotypes.

However, as therapeutic targets move toward increasingly lower LDL-C levels, it is crucial to integrate broader lipid-cardiovascular dynamics into the clinical interpretation. Recent evidence has highlighted paradoxical associations in certain settings, such as the reported link between lower LDL-C levels and a higher incidence of atrial fibrillation Hayıroğlu et al. (2023) [40]. A meta-analysis of nearly 4 million patients found that higher total cholesterol levels were inversely associated with AF risk, and notably, increased LDL-C levels were associated with a 7% reduction in the development of AF (HR = 0.93; p = 0.04). Although these findings are characterized by high heterogeneity, addressing such paradoxes strengthens the contextual understanding of aggressive lipid modulation in patients with complex cardiovascular phenotypes.

In contrast to the marked reductions of serum LDL, the pooled analysis demonstrated no statistically significant effect of PCSK9 inhibitor therapy on serum HDL levels which is consistent with the established mechanism of action of PCSK9 inhibitors as it primarily enhance the clearance of atherogenic lipoproteins via the LDL receptor pathway and are not known to directly influence the complex metabolism of HDL particles [41]. The neutral effect on HDL, observed alongside significant clinical benefit, delineate that the cardioprotective and limb-salvage effects of PCSK9 inhibition are mediated almost exclusively through the substantial depletion of apolipoprotein B-containing lipoproteins rather than through modulation of HDL.The significant reduction in triglycerides unmasked by sensitivity analysis constitutes an additional, noteworthy finding. This suggests a variable pharmacodynamic effect on triglyceride levels, potentially influenced by baseline concentrations or other patient-specific determinants. This finding is supported by results from the DESCARTES trial, a dedicated study of Evolocumab which reported a significant reduction in triglyceride levels alongside its primary LDL-lowering effect [42]. The mechanism for this effect is considered an indirect consequence of robust LDL receptor upregulation, which enhances the hepatic catabolism of triglyceride-rich lipoprotein remnants [43]. This ancillary reduction in triglyceride-rich lipoproteins may thereby contribute to a decrease in overall atherogenic burden, particularly among patients with mixed dyslipidemia.A significant reduction in Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events (MACE) was observed which substantiates the cardiovascular protective effects of PCSK9 inhibition specifically within the high-risk peripheral artery disease population. This finding corroborates and extends evidence from SPIRE-2 trial, which demonstrated consistent risk reduction pattern in patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease [38]. A notable finding was a significant reduction in major amputation (RR = 0.38). However, this result is based on a limited number of events, and the confidence interval is wide, indicating considerable uncertainty. While pathophysiologically plausible -as intensive lipid-lowering is hypothesized to stabilize vulnerable plaques within the limb vasculature, thereby preventing acute thrombotic occlusions that precipitate critical limb-threatening ischemia [31] and supported by emerging evidence that PCSK9 inhibition may favorably modulate microcirculatory function [44], this finding should be viewed as preliminary and hypothesis-generating rather than conclusive. The profound implications of limb loss for mortality, morbidity, and quality of life [45] underscore the importance of confirming this signal in larger trials. The critical importance of lipid management for limb preservation is recognized in the European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS) Clinical Practice Guidelines which advocate for aggressive LDL lowering to reduce major adverse limb events [46]. Similarly, the reductions in MACE and MI, while statistically significant, are derived from a modest number of events and should be interpreted with caution. The non-significant findings for revascularization and all-cause mortality likely reflect insufficient statistical power and the relatively short follow-up durations in the included studies, rather than a true absence of effect.

Beyond physiological efficacy, a major clinical barrier to successful lipid therapy remains patient adherence. Real-world data consistently demonstrate that suboptimal adherence to daily statin regimens markedly reduces the expected cardiovascular benefits (Özdemir et al., 2017). PCSK9 inhibitors characterized by their infrequent dosing schedule and high efficacy may offer a significant advantage in improving long-term therapeutic adherence compared to traditional daily oral therapies. Incorporating these practical dimensions is essential for a contemporary interpretation of the role of PCSK9 inhibitors in managing high-risk PAD populations [47].

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: First, the body of evidence is derived mainly from subgroup analyses of large cardiovascular trials rather than studies prospectively powered for PAD-specific endpoints. While this provides high-quality efficacy data for major adverse cardiovascular events, it may limit the precision of estimates for limb-specific outcomes and the generalizability to patients with isolated PAD. Additionally, the included studies used varying diagnostic criteria for PAD (e.g., ABI thresholds, clinical symptoms) and enrolled patients across a spectrum of disease severity, which may affect the applicability of our findings to specific PAD phenotypes in clinical practice. Second, we observed substantial statistical heterogeneity (I2 > 50%) for several key endpoints, including flow-mediated dilation, HDL, and MACE. While we employed a random-effects model to account for this and explored sources clinically, the residual heterogeneity indicates that the pooled estimates for these outcomes should be interpreted with an understanding that the true effect may vary across different PAD subpopulations and study methodologies. Third, the interpretation of these findings must be circumscribed by several limitations. The paucity of included studies, n = 6, constrained the statistical power for subgroup analyses and precluded a reliable assessment of publication bias. Significant heterogeneity was detected for several endpoints, indicative of underlying clinical and methodological diversity that could not be fully explored. The incorporation of a single non-randomized study introduces the potential for residual confounding. Finally, the preponderance of short-term follow-up data limits the ability to draw definitive conclusions regarding long-term clinical outcomes such as mortality.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: PCSK9 inhibitors in peripheral artery disease significantly reduce LDL cholesterol and carotid intima-media thickness, and may reduce the risk of major adverse cardiovascular events, myocardial infarction, and major amputation. However, these clinical outcome findings are based on limited event data and require confirmation in larger, dedicated trials. No clear effects were observed on ankle-brachial index, flow-mediated dilation, revascularization, or all-cause mortality. These findings highlight a role in plaque stabilization and event prevention rather than rapid hemodynamic change. Given residual risk and frequent statin limitations in peripheral artery disease, PCSK9 inhibitors represent a valuable adjunct to guideline-based therapy.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Khadeeja Ali Hamzah: Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yousif Hameed Kurmasha: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Mohamed Fouad Abdrabo: Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Mohamed F. Srour: Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration. Ali Saad Al-Shammari: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Mohammed Hamed Ibrahium Badi: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mohammedsadeq A. Shweliya: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Nihar Jena: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Yasar Sattar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

sec.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Consent for publication
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: No funding was received for this study.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

sec.p: Data supporting the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
